title: Try the Oldest Known Recipe For Toothpaste: From Ancient Egypt, Circa the 4th Century BC
description:
Image of Ancient EgyptôÙian DenôÙtistry, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
When we assume that modôÙern improveôÙments are far supeôÙriôÙor to the pracôÙtices of
body:
Image of Ancient EgyptôÙian DenôÙtistry, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
When we assume that modôÙern improveôÙments are far supeôÙriôÙor to the pracôÙtices of the ancients, we might do well to actuôÙalôÙly learn how peoôÙple in the disôÙtant past lived before indulging in ãchronoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal snobôÙbery.ã Take, for examôÙple, the area of denôÙtal hygiene. We might imagôÙine the ancient Greeks or EgypôÙtians as prone to ramôÙpant tooth decay, lackôÙing the benôÙeôÙfits of packôÙaged, brandôÙed toothôÙpaste, silken ribôÙbons of floss, astrinôÙgent mouthôÙwash, and ergonomôÙic toothôÙbrushôÙes. But in fact, as toothôÙpaste manôÙuôÙfacôÙturôÙer ColôÙgate points out, ãthe basic funôÙdaôÙmenôÙtalsã of toothôÙbrush design ãhave not changed since the times of the EgypôÙtians and Babyloniansãa hanôÙdle to grip, and a brisôÙtle-like feaôÙture with which to clean the teeth.ã And not only did ancient peoôÙple use toothôÙbrushôÙes, but it is believed that ãEgypôÙtiansãÎ startôÙed using a paste to clean their teeth around 5000 BC,ã even before toothôÙbrushôÙes were inventôÙed.
In 2003, curaôÙtors at a VienôÙnese museôÙum disôÙcovôÙered ãthe worldãs oldôÙest-known forôÙmuôÙla for toothôÙpaste,ã writes Irene Zoech in The TeleôÙgraph, ãused more than 1,500 years before ColôÙgate began marôÙketôÙing the first comôÙmerôÙcial brand in 1873.ã DatôÙing from the 4th cenôÙtuôÙry AD, the EgyptôÙian papyrus (not shown above), writôÙten in Greek, describes a ãpowôÙder for white and perôÙfect teethã that, when mixed with saliôÙva, makes a ãclean tooth paste.ã The recipe is as folôÙlows, Zoech sumôÙmaôÙrizes: ããÎone drachôÙma of rock saltãmeasure equal to one hunôÙdredth of an ounceãtwo drachôÙmas of mint, one drachôÙma of dried iris flower and 20 grains of pepôÙper, all of them crushed and mixed togethôÙer.ã
Zoech quotes DenôÙtist Heinz NeuôÙman, who remarked, ãNobody in the denôÙtal proôÙfesôÙsion had any idea that such an advanced toothôÙpaste forôÙmuôÙla of this antiqôÙuiôÙty existôÙed.ã HavôÙing tried the ancient recipe at a denôÙtal conôÙferôÙence in AusôÙtria, he found it ãnot unpleasôÙantã
It was painful on my gums and made them bleed as well, but thatãs not a bad thing, and afterôÙwards my mouth felt fresh and clean. I believe that this recipe would have been a big improveôÙment on some of the soap toothôÙpastes used much latôÙer.
DisôÙcovôÙered among ãthe largest colôÙlecôÙtion of ancient EgyptôÙian docôÙuôÙments in the world,ã the docôÙuôÙment, says HerôÙmann HarôÙrauer, head of the papyrus colôÙlecôÙtion at the NationôÙal Library in VienôÙna, ãwas writôÙten by someôÙone whoãs obviôÙousôÙly had some medôÙical knowlôÙedge, as he used abbreôÙviôÙaôÙtions for medôÙical terms.ã
When we surôÙveyô the denôÙtal remeôÙdies of Medieval EngôÙland, we do indeed find that modôÙern denôÙtal care is far betôÙter thanô much of what was availôÙable then. Most denôÙtal cures of the time, writes Trevor AnderôÙson in a Nature artiôÙcle, ãwere based on herbal remeôÙdies, charms and amulets.ã For examôÙple, inô the 1314ô Rosa AngliôÙca, writer John of GadôÙdesôÙden reports, ãsome say that the beak of a magôÙpie hung from the neck cures pain in the teeth.ã AnothôÙer remôÙeôÙdy involves stickôÙing a neeôÙdle into a ãmany footôÙed worm which rolls up in a ball when you touch it.ã Touch the aching tooth with that roly-poly neeôÙdle and ãthe pain will be erased.ã
HowôÙevôÙer, ãthere is also docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary eviôÙdence,ã writes AnderôÙson, ãfor powôÙders to clean teeth and attempts at fillôÙing carôÙiôÙous cavôÙiôÙties,ã as well as some surôÙgiôÙcal interôÙvenôÙtion. In GilberôÙtus AngliôÙcusã 13th cenôÙtuôÙry ComôÙpendiôÙum of MedôÙiôÙcine, readôÙers are told to rub teeth and gums with cloth after eatôÙing to ensure that ãno corôÙrupt matôÙter abides among the teeth.ã In The TroôÙtuôÙlaãa comôÙpendiôÙum of folk remeôÙdies from the 11th or 12th centuryãwe find many recipes for what we might conôÙsidôÙer toothôÙpaste, though their effiôÙcaôÙcy is dubiôÙous. Daniû´le CybulôÙskie at Medievalists.net quotes one recipe ãfor black teethã:
ãÎtake walôÙnut shells well cleaned of the inteôÙriôÙor rind, which is green, andãÎ rub the teeth three times a day, and when they have been well rubbedãÎ wash the mouth with warm wine, and with salt mixed if desired.
AnothôÙer, more extravôÙaôÙgant, recipe sounds impracôÙtiôÙcal.
Take burnt white marôÙble and burnt date pits, and white natron, a red tile, salt, and pumice. From all of these make a powôÙder in which damp wool has been wrapped in a fine linen cloth. Rub the teeth inside and out.
Yet a third recipe gives us a luxôÙuôÙry variôÙety, its ingreôÙdiôÙents well out of reach of the averôÙage perôÙson. We are assured, howôÙevôÙer, that this forôÙmuôÙla ãworks the best.ã
Take some each of cinôÙnaôÙmon, clove, spikeôÙnard, masôÙtic, frankôÙinôÙcense, grain, wormôÙwood, crab foot, date pits, and olives. Grind all of these and reduce them to a powôÙder, then rub the affectôÙed places.
Whether any of these forôÙmuôÙlasô would have worked at all, I canôÙnot say, but they likeôÙly worked betôÙter than charms and amulets. In any case, whileô medieval EuroôÙpean texts tend to conôÙfirm cerôÙtain of our ideas about poor denôÙtal hygiene of the past, it seems that the daiôÙly pracôÙtices of more ancient peoôÙples in Egypt and elseôÙwhere might have been much more like our own thanô we would susôÙpect.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Why the Ancient Romans Had BetôÙter Teeth Than ModôÙern EuroôÙpeans
Who RealôÙly Built the EgyptôÙian PyramidsãAnd How Did They Do It?
DisôÙcovôÙer the OldôÙest Beer Recipe in HisôÙtoôÙry From Ancient SumeôÙria, 1800 B.C.
An Ancient EgyptôÙian HomeôÙwork AssignôÙment from 1800 Years Ago: Some Things Are TruôÙly TimeôÙless
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The $666 Board That Built Apple: How the Apple I Changed Computing 50 Years Ago
description:
in History, Technology |   	April 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

AmerôÙiôÙcans of a cerôÙtain age may well rememôÙber growôÙing up with an Apple I
body:
in History, Technology |   	April 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

AmerôÙiôÙcans of a cerôÙtain age may well rememôÙber growôÙing up with an Apple II in the classôÙroom, and the perôÙpetôÙuôÙal tempôÙtaôÙtion it held out to play The OreôÙgon Trail,ô NumôÙber MunchôÙers, or perôÙhapsô Lode RunôÙner. More than a few recess gamers went on to comôÙputôÙer-oriôÙentôÙed careers, but only the most curiôÙous sought an answer to the quesôÙtion implied in the machineãs name: was there an Apple I? Half a cenôÙtuôÙry after the founôÙdaôÙtion of Apple, Inc., then known as Apple ComôÙputôÙer, the prodôÙuct that launched whatãs now one of the worldãs most valuôÙable comôÙpaôÙnies remains very much an obscuôÙriôÙty. Unless you freôÙquent comôÙputôÙer museôÙums, youãre unlikeôÙly ever to have laid eyes on an Apple I, let alone used one. Even if one of the existôÙing modôÙels were to come on the marôÙket, youãd need about half a milôÙlion dolôÙlars to buy it.


	

Itãs actuôÙalôÙly easôÙiôÙer to buy the parts that went into an Apple I and build it yourôÙself ã which, as demonôÙstratôÙed by the 8ãBit Guy in the video above, still isnãt easy at all. Yet it does conôÙvey someôÙthing of what Appleãs very first cusôÙtomers would have expeôÙriôÙenced in 1976, when do-it-yourôÙself was the order of the day in comôÙputôÙing.
When I bought the MacôÙBook on which Iãm writôÙing this post, I simôÙply opened it up and, natôÙuôÙralôÙly, found it ready to use. That would scarceôÙly have been imagôÙinôÙable to comôÙputôÙer enthuôÙsiôÙasts of the mid-sevôÙenôÙties, accusôÙtomed as they were to solôÙderôÙing indiôÙvidôÙuôÙalôÙly purôÙchased chips onto elecôÙtronôÙics boards by hand. The Apple I marked a great leap forôÙward in conôÙveôÙnience by comôÙing already assemôÙbled, albeit withôÙout a monôÙiôÙtor, a keyôÙboard, or even a case; the purôÙchase price of USD $666.66 (closôÙer to $4,000 today) just got you the board. But what a board.

Though we rememôÙber Steve Jobs as the masôÙterôÙmind, the Apple I is a tour de force of the engiôÙneerôÙing genius of his busiôÙness partôÙner Steve WozôÙniôÙak. When the Steves debuted it at the HomeôÙbrew ComôÙputôÙer Club in July of 1976, the relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly small numôÙber of chips and advanced funcôÙtions (BASIC proôÙgramôÙming! CasôÙsette-tape data storôÙage! ActuôÙal video outôÙput, if only of teleôÙtype-like scrolling text!) creôÙatôÙed a conôÙsidôÙerôÙable demand then and there. We often hear of Jobs and WozôÙniôÙak startôÙing Apple in a garage, and it was in that garage (as well as the houseôÙãs livôÙing room) that the first Apple I boards were put togethôÙer. UltiôÙmateôÙly, 200 were sold before the Apple II arrived the folôÙlowôÙing year. Appleãs first comôÙputôÙer may look intimôÙiôÙdatôÙing to most of todayãs Mac users. But conôÙsidôÙer the comôÙpaôÙnyãs repôÙuôÙtaôÙtion for minôÙiôÙmalôÙism, accesôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty, and a knack for capôÙturôÙing the conôÙsumerãs imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion: all qualôÙiôÙties present on that board 50 years ago.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
RidôÙley Scott on the MakôÙing of Appleãs IconôÙic ã1984ã ComôÙmerôÙcial, Aired on Super Bowl SunôÙday in 1984
Before The SimpôÙsons, Matt GroenôÙing IllusôÙtratôÙed a ãStudentãs Guideã for Apple ComôÙputôÙers (1989)
Hunter S. Thompsonãs Edgy 1990s ComôÙmerôÙcial for Appleãs MacôÙinôÙtosh ComôÙputôÙer: A MedôÙiôÙtaôÙtion on PowôÙer
DisôÙcovôÙered: The User ManôÙuôÙal for the OldôÙest SurôÙvivôÙing ComôÙputôÙer in the World
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Amid Urban Spaces, Alex Senna's Bold Murals Embrace Connection and Belonging
description: In the large-scale murals of Alex Senna, figures gather, greet one another, relax, and interact with their own shadows in bold compositions. The B
body:
In the large-scale murals of Alex Senna, figures gather, greet one another, relax, and interact with their own shadows in bold compositions. The Brazilian artist is known for his black-and-white murals that emphasize community and emotional bonds. Togetherness, security, and positivity pervade the scenes, sometimes playful and other times more contemplative. Set against colorful backgrounds and amid urban structures, Sennaãs pieces emphasize connection, support, reflection, and belonging.
At the end of May, Senna embarks on a tour across Italy, France, and Spain to participate in several festivals. Follow the artistãs Instagram for updates.
Festival Monstar, Bosnia (2022). Photo by Ilda Kero
ãAcrobatasã (2022), Festival da Paz, Friedensstadt, Augsburg. Photo by Sebastian Buehler
ãA Estrela de Matusalûˋmã (2022), Festival Gragnano, Italy. Photo by Noemi Verdoliva
Festival Civita Street Fest, Italy. Photo by Alessia Di Risio
Festival Stadt Wand Kunst, Germany (2024). Photo by The Realness
ãSolitariosã (2024). Photo by Pedro Bayeux
ãAtento porûˋm Dispersoã (2024), Kansas City
ãBaile dos Mascaradosã (2022). Photo by Erib

title: 'Our Neighbors, the Peacocks' Paints a Portrait of an Unusual Convergence of Populations
description: When we think of ãinvasive species,ã perhaps zebra mussels or kudzu vine spring to mind. Both have flourished in their non-native environments and
body:
When we think of ãinvasive species,ã perhaps zebra mussels or kudzu vine spring to mind. Both have flourished in their non-native environments and continue to threaten other native organisms. Invasive species arenãt inherently badãtheyãre just trying to surviveãbut by definition, theyãre likely to disrupt local ecosystems and even cause billions of dollars worth of damage each year. So, what does one California city have to say about its burgeoning population ofãÎ peacocks?
Introduced by a businessman and land baron named Elias Lucky Baldwin more than a century ago, the avian population has long called the area home. Over the years, though, as the originally open area filled with homes and commercial strips, efforts by local residents end up at odds, as some would like to protect the birds and others would prefer to see them sent away altogether. In a short film titled ãOur Neighbors, the Peacocks,ã filmmaker Callie Barlow traces the unusual history of peafowl in Los Angeles County through the eyes of some of its current residents.



Arcadia, California, sits in the San Gabriel Valley about 45 minutes from downtown Los Angeles. Dozens of peacocks, which are carefully watched over by some and detested by others, meander through residentsã yards, traipse around on rooftops, peck at cars, call from trees, and of course, display their beautiful feathersãespecially during mating season.
In her short documentary, Barlow invites locals to share their loveãor loathingãfor the vibrant birds as she highlights how Arcadiaãs history of protecting the birds has perhaps led to something of an overpopulation problem. Nearby neighborhoods participate in relocation programs, while Arcadiaãs birds are protected, and opinions about how to deal with growing numbers land all along the spectrum.
ããOur Neighbors, the Peacocksã resists the urge to resolve the tension it so clearly lays out,ã says Jason Sondhi, who selected the film for Short of the Week. ãInstead, it leans into a modest but resonant idea articulated by its director that living alongside these animals might require ãputting aside your own discomforts to find a deeper meaning in nature.'ã
See the film on YouTube.

schema.headline: 'Our Neighbors, the Peacocks' Paints a Portrait of an Unusual Convergence of Populations

title: Art by Graphic Rewilding Blooms at Brookfield Place in New York City
description:
Announcement
                Bold and vibrant large-scale installations featuring blossoming flowers celebrate the natural world
body:
Announcement
                Bold and vibrant large-scale installations featuring blossoming flowers celebrate the natural world and bring the outside indoors. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Installation view of Fleeting Opulence by Graphic Rewilding at Brookfield Place (photo by Jakob Dahlin)
    
        
            Visit Brookfield Place and experienceô Fleeting Opulence by British artist duoô Graphic Rewilding. The bold and vibrant large-scale installations transform the Winter Garden and surrounding spaces into an immersive celebration of the natural world. On view through October 2026, the work features a variety of larger-than-life flowers and cherry blossoms in full bloom that shift with the changing light, creating an ever-evolving environment. Both joyful and contemplative, the installation invites the audience to look up, slow down, and experience nature woven into the fabric of the city.Graphic Rewilding is the 2026 recipient of the Brookfield Place New York Annual Arts Commission. Founded by Catherine Borowskiô andô Lee Baker, the art collectiveãs mission is to transform urban spaces with their vast, nature-inspired, immersive art.Brookfield Place is located at 230 Vesey Street in Lower Manhattan. When you visit, make sure to experience the world-class shopping and dining options available at this iconic New York destination.Learn more and plan your visit at bfplny.com.
                  
                      
                        Announcement
                      
                      
                        Sponsored
                      
                      
                        New York

title: Jule Korneffel Finds Meaning at the End of Light
description:
Art Review
                Her paintings compress Roman mythology, Italian Renaissance paintings, color relationships, and that
body:
Art Review
                Her paintings compress Roman mythology, Italian Renaissance paintings, color relationships, and that moment before disappearance. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Jule Korneffel, "In Search of Lost Light" (2025), natural pigments, acrylic on canvas (all photos courtesy Spencer Brownstone Gallery)
    
        
            Since I first saw Jule Korneffelãs acrylic and natural pigment paintings at her thesis exhibition at Hunter in 2018, I have watched an increasing gravitas enter her work in debut and subsequent exhibitions at Spencer Brownstone Gallery in 2019 and 2022, and a 2025 group exhibition I curated that included her work.ô Korneffel develops a palette for each painting based on research and intuition. She pays particular attention to the paintãs viscosity and its capacity for making distinct kinds of marks.ô One of the engaging paradoxes of her work is the relationship between austerity and lushness, restraint and declaration. The marks ãô their shape and thickness ã seem crucial and spontaneous, while carrying a trace of whimsy and vulnerability.Korneffelãs work has become more exquisitely nuanced as she pursues her interest in light, from strong to fading. In her most recent exhibition, In Search of Lost Light, at Spencer Brownstone Gallery, on view through May 2, she started out wanting to make a group of blue paintings but ended up working on gray paintings. This was due to her circumstances ã she spent the winter living in northeastern Germany and reading Marcel Proust, who (like Korneffel) was interested in Claude Monet. The winter sky of this region, where Berlin is situated and Korneffelãs family lives, is a dense fog in which the sun briefly appears, suspended like a pale yellow orb barely visible behind a dusky gray veil.Installation view of Jule Korneffel, "New York City Rising" (2026), acrylic and natural pigments on brick wallThere are seven paintings ranging between 20 x 18 inches (~51 x 56 cm) and 80 x 96 inches (~2 x 2.4 m), dated between 2023 and this year, in the exhibition. Outside, on the white concrete wall, the artist has painted a large, layered blue square, ãNew York City Rising.ã Around it, we see evidence of the colors she used in earlier layers. Sitting with it for a while, I watched the color change according to the ambient natural light and felt a connection between her work and that of James Turrell. Both are interested in light as a changing phenomenon, at once palpable and elusive.ãAurora and her Siblingsô (MorgenrûÑte)ã (2024) refers to the Roman myth of Aurora, who is the goddess of dawn, and sister of Sol (the Sun) and Luna (the Moon). Aurora announces the beginning of a new day. Against a layered, matte, dusty rose ground, we see two orbs,ô one gray and the other a dusty blue-violet. The gray orb is in the upper left-hand corner and the dusty blue-violet is in the center of the nearly square rectangle. Meanwhile, the titleãs parenthetical, "MorgenrûÑte," is German for ãmorning red.ã The painting compresses Roman mythology, Italian Renaissance paintings, color relationships, and that moment between appearance and disappearance.ô Looking at it, we see into the dense, layered, slightly textured surface and glimpse other colors which seem to fade before our eyes.Jule Korneffel, "Morning Star" (2025), natural pigments, acrylic on canvasThe title of ãWithered Summer (Proust) / Giottoãs San Francis before restorationã (2023) establishes the field of research, connections, and memories that went into the making of the painting. The gray ground and gray-green orbs and marks refer to Giottoãs ãStories of St. Francisã (c. 1297ã1300) frescoes in Florenceãs Santa Croce (Bardi Chapel) and Assisiãs Basilica of St. Francis before their cloudy, badly damaged surfaces were cleaned, removing years of dirt and soot. Korneffel, who saw them before they were restored, connects her experience to Proustãs theme of the ãwithered summerã and fading, melancholic moments when autumn is approaching.ô Melancholia is a theme running through these paintings, a feeling of time passing and darkness becoming unavoidable, and what that means. Instead of lamenting this state, Korneffel both memorializes and celebrates the fading of the light, the rising of each new day.ô Jule Korneffel: In Search of Lost Light continues at Spencer Brownstone Gallery (70-A Suffolk Street, Lower East Side, Manhattan) through May 2. The exhibition was organized by the gallery. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Jule Korneffel
                      
                      
                        Spencer Brownstone Gallery
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: Jule Korneffel Finds Meaning at the End of Light

title: Ai Weiwei Wrote the Book on Censorship
description:
Books Newsletter
                Plus, inside a Black Panther family album, predatory art-world relationships, and the unknown Qing D
body:
Books Newsletter
                Plus, inside a Black Panther family album, predatory art-world relationships, and the unknown Qing Dynasty trade portraitists. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            ãHow long can you silence the very thing that makes you human?ã asks our Editor-in-Chief Hakim Bishara in his review of Ai Weiweiãs new book On Censorship. The dissident Chinese artistãs ãsmall but mightyã book draws on a lifetime of fighting state control and packs in ever timely reflections on the harms of censorship ãô not just in authoritarian regimes, but also in the so-called enlightened West. Also in this edition: a sojourn inside a Black Panther family album, a peek into the lives of the now-anonymous painters in the Qing dynasty Canton trade system, and a semi-autobiographical novel about a predatory art teacher. ãLisa Yin Zhang, associate editorãJoju & Maceo [Cleaver], Hydra 1970ã (ôˋ Kathleen Neal Cleaver Archive; album photo John Stephens, image courtesy Kathleen Neal Cleaver Archive)Inside a Black Panther Family AlbumKathleen and Eldridge Cleaverãs family album depicts aspirational homemaking in diaspora, capturing the tension between rest and motion as they navigated exile with their children. | Leigh Raiford
From Our CriticsAi Weiwei and the Art of Keeping Your Mouth ShutOn Censorship offers timely reflections from the dissident artist, whose entire life and career have been marked by state persecution. | Hakim BisharaOn Censorshipô (2026) by Ai WeiweiThe Unnameable Artists of the Canton Trade SystemIn a book on Qing-era trade portraitists whose names are lost to history, Winnie Wong shows us how our restless pursuits of authenticity guide us into pitfalls of our own making. | Nanase ShirokawaThe Many Names of Anonymity: Portraitists of the Canton Tradeô (2026) by Winnie WongIn Discipline, Larissa Pham Explores Predatory Art-World MentorshipThe art critic and former painter reinvents the genreãs well-trod territory in her debut novel, which makes heartbreakingly acute the consequences of teacher-student relationships. | Claudia RossDiscipline: A Novelô (2026) by Larissa PhamFrom the ArchiveDavid and Stephen Hunter pose near a television set, c. 1960ã1970 (courtesy Ten Speed Press/Penguin Random House)Documenting the Black History Not Taught in ClassroomsThe photographs in Renata Cherliseãs Black Archives capture Black people experiencing moments of love, joy, rest, leisure, and everyday life. | Briana Ellis-Gibbs
                  
                      
                        Books Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

schema.headline: Ai Weiwei Wrote the Book on Censorship
